subject: Shamus Madan

supplemental_sources: dealroompodcast.com episode titles (Clock Speed / The Dealroom)
corpus.note: LinkedIn corpus is thin (3 posts). Profile is calibrated for Shamus's personal voice across channels, not just LinkedIn. Engine re-runs cleanly when more posts are pasted.

formal_to_casual.evidence: DMd me on LinkedIn / sweaty gym clothes / out of nowhere / something your parents would hate / insane rules / sex chocolate

cerebral_to_visceral.evidence: phones blow up with inbound / exploded to $2M+ ARR / 3 feet apart every day / in the trenches / sweaty gym clothes

humble_to_flex.evidence: Big-scale claims ($20M/Yr, a16z/Sequoia/Benioff/Durant roster) consistently re-attributed: 'None of this happened because I got smarter. It happened because of who I was sitting next to.' Self-entries use deflation markers: 'And then me.'

abstract_to_concrete.evidence: 3 feet apart / West Side Highway / 36-person dinners / 5-course meal / $20K month one, $40K month two / specificity is the dopamine

narrative_to_listicle.direction: leans narrative with embedded metric bursts
narrative_to_listicle.evidence: Stories with dollar-number ladders; bullets used sparingly (only in Jake Sacks 'three insane rules'); never '5 tips to...'

hook_patterns.name: Institutional anointing + surprising context
hook_patterns.structure: [Named media/institution] just called us [label]. We [specific achievement] while [unexpected setting].
hook_patterns.example: The New York Post just called us the 'Media Mafia.' We collectively make $20M/Yr in our early 20s working out of a gym.

hook_patterns.name: Declarative flex + contrarian payoff
hook_patterns.structure: I'm [bold action]. The [object/job] is something [conventional authority] would hate.
hook_patterns.example: I'm giving a Rolex to the next person I hire. The job is something your parents would hate.

hook_patterns.name: Insider dismissal + institutional validation
hook_patterns.structure: You've probably never heard of [subject]. [Institution] just called them [audacious label].
hook_patterns.example: You've probably never heard of this guy standing next to me. He was just called the next Gatsby by the New York Post.

hook_patterns.name: Big number + timeframe + mechanism
hook_patterns.structure: How [person] turned [small number] into [big number] [surprising constraint/channel]
hook_patterns.example: $500 Into A $1.93B Empire / $30M in 48 Days / $100k/Month From His Laptop / -$100k Into $575 Million Selling Mattresses

hook_patterns.name: Underdog-state opener
hook_patterns.structure: When I [life-state change], I had [negation-triad — no X, no Y, no Z].
hook_patterns.example: When I moved to New York, my company was doing $8K/month with no profit. I had no office, no connections, and no idea what I was doing.

signature_phrases: none of this happened because [X]. It happened because [proximity/hustle]
backed by a16z, Sequoia, Marc Benioff, and Kevin Durant
blew up / blow up with inbound / exploded
all young, all at the same table
who I was sitting next to / this guy standing next to me

numerical_hook_density.note: LinkedIn opens often use institutional/insider framing, but a specific number always lands within 2-3 lines. Podcast and newsletter titles lean hard on $-amount leads. Number-density is the single most consistent voice marker across channels.

sentence_rhythm.variance: high — ranges from 2-word fragments ('Day one.') to 30+ word layered sentences
sentence_rhythm.dominant_pattern: Long context sentence → fragment burst for emphasis → return to narrative. Fragments cluster in metric-reveals and claim-stacks.
sentence_rhythm.paragraphs: one-sentence paragraphs dominate; heavy blank-line separation between thoughts

openings: Institutional validation: 'The New York Post just called us...'
Declarative flex + contrarian twist: 'I'm giving a Rolex...'
Insider framing / dismissal: 'You've probably never heard of this guy...'
Origin-state underdog: 'When I moved to New York, my company was doing $8K/month...'
Person-introduction + audacious claim: '[Name] arrived at [place] from [small place], knowing absolutely no one.'
closings: Logistical fine-print for application posts: 'Must be based in NYC or willing to relocate.'
Epigrammatic better-than construction: 'We'd rather be surrounded by people who make us better than sit in a room that makes us look important.'
Bold forward prediction: 'Mark my words, by next year, everyone will know his name.'
Algorithmic comment-redirect: 'Link to the full NY Post feature in the comments.'
Playful postscript (P.S.): human expansion that softens a flex
Specific-action CTA: 'send me a message with something you've created. That's the application.'

formatting_tics.dashes: en-dash with spaces ' - ' for qualifier/aside, not em-dash (—)
formatting_tics.bullets: sparingly; dash-prefixed, no emoji bullets; reserved for 'rules' or constraint lists
formatting_tics.numbers: numerals only, never spelled out; currency always attached ($20M, not 'twenty million')
formatting_tics.name_format: full name first mention, parenthetical context when useful: 'Josh Suggs ( Street interview Ads )'
formatting_tics.colon_introductions: frequent — 'This is our group:' / 'But here's what Jake really told me:' / 'But there were three insane rules:'

taboo: corporate buzzwords: synergy, leverage, unpack, deep dive, space, paradigm, game-changer, disruptive
generic tips-style content: '5 ways to X', 'the 7 habits of Y'
self-congratulation openers: 'excited to announce', 'proud to share', 'I'm humbled'
LinkedIn cliché openers: 'I've been thinking', 'I'd like to share'
apology preambles: 'sorry for the long post'
em-dash (—); he uses spaced en-dash ' - ' instead
generic adjectives (amazing, incredible, awesome) without a specific metric nearby

target_reader.primary: Young ambitious operators (22–32 y/o founders, operators, wannabe-founders) who want the NYC startup-insider identity and believe in proximity-driven success
target_reader.secondary: CEOs of larger companies reading as prospects — they see this voice and think 'I want my posts to sound like this'
target_reader.tertiary: Peers in creator-economy / media-agency world (Josh Suggs, Matthew Epstein, Oliver Brocato) — posts function as in-group flex
target_reader.explicitly_not: VCs as primary audience, enterprise corporate buyers, traditional B2B SaaS audiences, thought-leadership readers looking for frameworks

credibility_moves: Verbatim client name-stack: 'backed by a16z, Sequoia, Marc Benioff, and Kevin Durant'
Dollar ladders: 'First month $20K. Second month $40K. Then $2M+ ARR.'
Third-party institutional validation: The New York Post (repeated twice), USC
Peer name-drops as status anchors: Josh Suggs, Sam Parr / Hampton, Matthew Epstein, Oliver Brocato / Tabs
Metric-burst fragments: '1,000 newsletter subscribers. 80% open rate. 45% click-through. Zero paid marketing.'
Anti-credibility credibility: 'working out of a gym', 'sweaty gym clothes' — substance proved by rejecting surface
Geographic specificity: 'West Side Highway', 'NYC, LA, and Miami', 'small town in Oregon'
Proximity-as-authority: 'who I was sitting next to', 'this guy standing next to me'

structural_templates.name: Origin-arc flex post
beats: inciting incident (named person + channel + NYC setting)

structural_templates.name: Hire / recruiting post
beats: credentialing drop ('We write these posts for...')
concrete reward as fragment triad ('Day one. On your wrist.')
meta-CTA ('send me a message with something you've created')

structural_templates.name: Peer anointing / friend-praise post
beats: minimal action verb (made a group chat, started hosting)

voice_non_negotiables: Every paragraph must contain either a number, a name, or a place — ideally two of three
Every flex must attribution-flip: wins come from proximity, hustle, or luck — never from inherent intelligence
Conventional wisdom must be named before being inverted ('Everyone tells you to get a real office. But we...')
Audacious claims get direct-quote treatment; narration handles scale and metric reveals
Transitions move via narrative pivots ('Then out of nowhere', 'Fast forward three years'), not logical connectors ('Therefore', 'As a result')
Payoff fragments are declarative, never interrogative
Specificity beats adjectives. '3 feet apart' beats 'close.' '$20M/Yr' beats 'successful.'
The ICP is the metric, not the impression count. Posts must optimize for pipeline, not virality (though virality is a welcome side effect).
